tasks.name: Git Backup
tasks.system_prompt: You are an automation agent responsible for performing git backups and notifying the user of results. Execute tasks reliably and report outcomes clearly.
tasks.prompt:
Execute the git-backup skill by running the backup script at /a0/usr/skills/git-backup/scripts/backup.py, then use the notify-user skill to send a notification with the backup result.

Steps:
1. Clean up all of the task history if the last run is successful. This is critical to keep our context minimal without losing important details!
2. Run: python /a0/usr/skills/git-backup/scripts/backup.py
3. Capture the output/result
4. Send notification using: python /a0/usr/skills/notify-user/scripts/notify.py "<backup result message>" --title "Git Backup Result" --priority 3
Always report whether the backup succeeded or failed in the notification.
tasks.last_result:

| Metric | Count |
|--------|-------|

| Failed backups | 0 |

📬 **Notification sent:** Result delivered via notify-user skill with priority 3

All four steps executed successfully. Context kept minimal as requested.

tasks.name: Fetching Jira Tickets
tasks.system_prompt: You are a reliable automation agent. Use skills_tool to load and follow skills. Execute commands precisely and report results.
tasks.prompt:
Load the jira skill using skills_tool and follow its instructions to fetch and cache Jira tickets with --filter-id 13811 --view-id 10489904.

Wait for the skill to complete fully (5-15 min). Do NOT interrupt it.

On completion, load the notify-user skill using skills_tool and send:

On failure, load the notify-user skill and send the error summary with --priority 4.

tasks.name: Blog Ideas
tasks.system_prompt: You are a blog topic researcher for Able.SG, a tech leadership blog by Michael Bui - a Singapore-based engineering leader who runs an extensive homelab, builds multi-agent AI systems, and leads AI transformation at a large retail organization.
tasks.prompt:
This task has 4 steps. Follow them in exact order.

== STEP 1: Extract Published Blog Titles and Date Range ==

Run this shell command to get published (non-draft) blog post titles AND the search date range:

bash -c '
REPO_DIR="/sources/github.com/MichaelBui/AbleSG"
if [ -d "$REPO_DIR/.git" ]; then
  git -C "$REPO_DIR" pull --quiet 2>/dev/null
else
  mkdir -p "$(dirname "$REPO_DIR")"
  git clone https://github.com/MichaelBui/AbleSG.git "$REPO_DIR" --depth 1 --quiet 2>/dev/null
fi
echo "=== PUBLISHED POSTS ==="
count=0
for f in "$REPO_DIR"/content/english/posts/*.md; do
  draft=$(grep -m1 "^draft:" "$f" 2>/dev/null | tr -d " " | cut -d: -f2)
  [ "$draft" = "true" ] && continue
  title=$(grep -m1 "^title:" "$f" 2>/dev/null | sed "s/^title: *//;s/\"//g")
  if [ -n "$title" ]; then
    echo "- $title"
    count=$((count + 1))
  fi
done
if [ "$count" -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "WARNING: No blog titles found. Check if /sources mount is available."
fi
echo ""
echo "=== SEARCH DATE RANGE ==="
THIS_MONTH="$(date +"%B %Y")"
PREV_MONTH="$(date -d "last month" +"%B %Y" 2>/dev/null || date -v-1m +"%B %Y" 2>/dev/null)"
echo "Search for topics trending in: $PREV_MONTH and $THIS_MONTH"
echo "⚠️ CRITICAL: All search queries must end with \" since $PREV_MONTH\" keywords"
'

Save the output. The PUBLISHED_POSTS section lists posts to avoid duplicating. The SEARCH DATE RANGE gives you two months to include in your search queries.

== STEP 2: Research Topics (run at least 7 web searches) ==

You must perform AT LEAST 7 separate web searches using search_engine before proposing topics. Design your own search queries, but follow these rules:

SEARCH REQUIREMENTS:
- Minimum 7 searches total, each with a DIFFERENT query
- At least 2 searches for Leadership topics (people management, engineering culture, AI adoption, trust, growth)
- At least 3 searches for Technology topics (agentic AI, local LLMs, homelab, MCP, clean architecture, DORA)
- At least 2 searches for Life topics (productivity, work-life balance, continuous learning, personal growth)
- Each search must approach the pillar from a DIFFERENT angle (e.g. for Technology: one about trends, one about community discussions, one about practical implementations)
- Include the month names and year from the SEARCH DATE RANGE (Step 1 output) in your queries to limit results to the last 2 months
- If a search returns interesting leads, do follow-up searches to go deeper

Execute all searches one by one, then analyze the results against Michael's expertise below.

MICHAEL'S UNIQUE EXPERTISE (real hands-on experience):
- Multi-agent orchestration (built 2 production systems: enterprise hackathon + homelab daily report)
- Agent Zero framework (runs 11 specialist agents, built custom theme plugin, skills for Gmail/Jira/GChat via CDP browser)
- AMD Strix Halo 128GB unified memory AI server (local LLM inference with ROCm/llama.cpp)
- Retail media networks / ad-tech (scaled from $1M to $10M+ GMV at NTUC FairPrice)
- DORA metrics, SLO management, engineering culture (Cycle Time <10d, CFR <2%, SLO >98%)
- AI adoption at organizational scale (drove 28% to 100% department-wide, 2/3 building agentic AI)
- Clean architecture in Go microservices (SOLID, 12-factor app, SonarCloud quality gates)
- Terraform IaC for homelab (Proxmox VMs, Docker containers, NFS)
- RAG evaluation (ColPali multi-vector vs sparse vector, error rates, normalization)
- Cost optimization ($100K+ annual cloud savings: Datadog $70K + GCP + SOLR legacy removal)
- Team leadership (up to 14 cross-functional members, mentored 5 engineers to team lead)
- Homelab migration (Kubernetes + ArgoCD + Cloudflare to Docker + Terraform + Traefik, 30% power reduction)
- Prompt engineering and context engineering for production agentic systems
- Vendor evaluation (CitrusAd, Mirakl, MoEngage, Algolia, theTradeDesk, OSMOS, Segment)

TOPIC SELECTION RULES:
1. Do NOT suggest topics overlapping with PUBLISHED_POSTS from Step 1
2. Each topic MUST connect to at least one item from Michael's expertise above
3. Prioritize topics where personal experience gives an angle generic AI content cannot replicate
4. No purely theoretical topics - must have a hands-on component
5. Target audience: mid-to-senior engineers, tech leads, engineering managers in APAC/Singapore
6. Each topic should be writable as 800-1200 words

After all searches are complete, decide on 5-7 topics. For each, pick a TITLE and a SCORE (1-10, composite of search demand + uniqueness + freshness). Keep these in mind for Step 3.

== STEP 3: Save Topics to File, Then Notify ==

⚠️ CRITICAL: This step has two parts, you MUST execute BOTH before moving to Step 4.

PART A - SAVE TO FILE:
Create the output directory and save all topics with full details to a markdown file. Run:

mkdir -p "workdir/Blog Ideas"

Then write a file at workdir/Blog Ideas/Topics_YYYY-MM-DD.md (replace YYYY-MM-DD with today's date). The file must follow this structure:

# Blog Topic Ideas - YYYY-MM-DD

## 1. [TITLE]
- **Pillar:** [Leadership / Technology / Life]
- **Score:** [X/10]
- **Angle:** [3-5 sentences on what makes Michael's take unique]
- **Trending Signal:** [detailed findings from web search with links to actual sources]
- **Related Expertise:** [which items from Michael's expertise list this connects to]

## 2. [TITLE]
(same structure for each topic)

PART B - SEND NOTIFICATION:
After saving the file, send a push notification with titles and scores. Execute:

python /a0/usr/skills/notify-user/scripts/notify.py "Blog Topics: (SCORE) TITLE | (SCORE) TITLE | (SCORE) TITLE | ... Check Agent Zero chat for details." --title "Blog Topic Ideas" --priority 3

Replace each SCORE and TITLE with actual values from your topics. Include ALL topics separated by " | ".

Execute both Part A and Part B now before proceeding to Step 4.

== STEP 4: Write Detailed Output ==

Now write the full details for each topic in this format:

[NUMBER]. TITLE: [proposed blog title]
ANGLE: [3-5 sentences - what personal experience or data makes Michael's take unique]
TRENDING SIGNAL: [what you found in web search - specific subreddit, HN thread, trend, or event, be detailed and verbose with links to actual references]
SCORE: [1-10]
tasks.last_result:
# Blog Topic Ideas - Detailed Analysis

## 1. From Vibe Coding to Production-Ready Agents: The Multi-Agent Gap That Breaks 90% of Teams
**ANGLE:** Most teams build toy agent demos but fail at production deployment due to missing infrastructure (state management, observability, graceful degradation). This post shares lessons from building 2 actual multi-agent systems in enterprise hackathon and homelab daily reports, focusing on the "3-6 months of engineering time" gap between frameworks like LangGraph/CrewAI and production-ready deployments. The unique angle: Michael's hands-on experience with Agent Zero (11 specialist agents) reveals why simple prompt chaining fails at scale.
**TRENDING SIGNAL:** DEV Community's "How to Build Multi-Agent Systems: Complete 2026 Guide" emphasizes critical patterns like "kill runaway processes before they drain your account." TrueFoundry's infrastructure article details requirements beyond just models: centralized agent registries, state management, and observability across entire action chains. Codebridge's March 2026 comparison of OpenClaw/Zapier/Make highlights security considerations for self-hosted vs managed SaaS. The gap between demo and production represents 3-6 months of engineering time not invested in core product.
**SCORE:** 9/10

## 2. The Trust Formula for Remote Engineering Teams in 2026: Outcomes Over Presence
**ANGLE:** Not another "how to build trust" guide, but specific patterns for measuring and building trust through outcome-based management in 2026's hybrid work reality. Covers the shift from surveillance to output measurement with concrete examples of how CTOs allocate budget without double-checking every engineering milestone. The unique angle: Michael's experience leading up to 14 cross-functional members and mentoring 5 engineers to team lead provides practical patterns for outcome-based trust.
**TRENDING SIGNAL:** Monday.com's "Building trust: how leaders create high trust teams in 2026" gives concrete example of marketing manager allocating budget without double-checking engineering milestones. Holloway Guide emphasizes outcomes over activity tracking. OZrit's March 2026 article notes the critical distinction between "managing presence vs enabling output." Engineering Manager Tools provides practical onboarding and informal touchpoint patterns for remote culture building.
**SCORE:** 8/10

## 3. Local LLM Inference on AMD Strix Halo: Real-World Costs vs Cloud ($10K+ Annual Savings)
**ANGLE:** Hands-on cost analysis of running local LLMs on AMD Strix Halo (128GB unified memory) vs cloud inference, with actual benchmarks from Phoronix and personal tests. Covers ROCm optimization, power consumption trade-offs, and when to self-host vs use cloud for specific workloads. The unique angle: Michael owns the exact hardware (AMD Strix Halo 128GB) and has direct experience running 122B-parameter models locally with ROCm/llama.cpp.
**TRENDING SIGNAL:** Phoronix benchmarks show ROCm 6.4.1 performance on Strix Halo, comparing against RTX 5090 (150-220+ TPS). Julien Simon's April 2026 buying guide lists DGX Spark at $4,699 (increased from $3,999 due to memory shortage), highlighting cost pressures. LinkedIn deep-dive by Pellegrini details actual 122B-parameter LLM running locally on AMD Strix Halo with real-world inference speeds and power consumption data. The platform offers lower raw throughput than M2 Ultra (~800 GB/s) but superior power efficiency for specific workloads.
**SCORE:** 10/10

## 4. MCP Security Patterns Every Engineering Leader Must Know: Beyond the Spec
**ANGLE:** Not just "what is MCP" but practical security patterns for production implementation. Covers OAuth conflicts with modern enterprise practices, session isolation requirements, tool permissions granularity, and network exposure risks. Includes decision framework for when to self-host vs use managed SaaS based on data sensitivity and compliance needs.
**TRENDING SIGNAL:** SentinelOne's complete guide on MCP security provides comprehensive control frameworks. Coalition for Secure AI whitepaper addresses "how do you secure autonomous systems that make real-time decisions and access sensitive resources." Red Hat notes OAuth specification conflicts with modern enterprise practices, requiring custom implementations. WorkOS March 2026 article covers everything teams need to know about transport scalability, agent communication governance, and enterprise readiness.
**SCORE:** 9/10

## 5. The Leadership Systems Problem: Why Burnout Prevention Fails and How to Fix It
**ANGLE:** Reframes burnout not as individual capacity problem but leadership systems failure. Covers how organizations mistake momentum for sustainability, activity for performance, with actionable patterns from evidence-based solutions. Unique angle: Michael's experience mentoring engineers through high-growth periods provides practical insights on when to intervene vs let teams self-correct.
**TREnding SIGNAL:** LinkedIn article "Burnout Isn't a Capacity Problem. It's a Leadership Systems Problem" by Jaime Taets (Feb 2026) establishes the core thesis. HRDConnect reports burnout rising again in 2026, requiring work redesign and thoughtful AI use rather than individual fixes. Circles.com notes treating burnout as strategic alarm shifts organizations from reaction to prevention mode. AllOne Consulting's evidence-based four-part approach shows how hundreds of organizations prevent burnout through systematic intervention.
**SCORE:** 8/10

## 6. AI Skills Gap in 2026: Why Training Isn't Enough and What Actually Works
**ANGLE:** Data-driven look at why most AI upskilling fails (only 15-20% workforce equipped with key AI skills per LinkedIn survey). Covers continuous learning loops, capability building vs course completion metrics, and how to build real workforce capability without burning out engineers. Unique angle: Michael drove 28% to 100% department-wide AI adoption through practical methods beyond traditional training.
**TRENDING SIGNAL:** DataCamp article "AI Skills Gap in 2026: Why Training Isn't Enough" (March 2026) details why most upskilling programs fail. BCG report notes AI will reshape more jobs than it replaces, requiring smaller population of senior engineering leaders with deep knowledge. ManpowerGroup March 2, 2026 report says AI skills officially most difficult to find globally. Talentful emphasizes rethinking talent strategy around adaptability, curiosity, and future capability rather than narrow technical expertise.
**SCORE:** 8/10

## 7. From Kubernetes to Docker + Terraform: The Homelab Migration That Cut Power by 30%
**ANGLE:** Detailed migration story from K8s + ArgoCD + Cloudflare to Docker + Terraform + Traefik, with actual power consumption data and cost analysis. Covers why simpler is sometimes better, when to use IaC for homelab infrastructure, and lessons learned from 30% power reduction. Unique angle: Michael's hands-on migration experience provides real metrics on operational complexity vs performance trade-offs.
**TRENDING SIGNAL:** Home lab community discussions increasingly favor Docker + Terraform over K8s for personal/homelab use cases (implied by search results on simplified stacks). Phoronix benchmarks show AMD Strix Halo power efficiency advantages when running optimized workloads. General 2026 trend toward cost-conscious infrastructure decisions driven by cloud pricing pressures and sustainability concerns.
**SCORE:** 9/10
